speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With Tom Wahl of Genesee, took place at his home in Pullman on March 10th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: One of my earliest. My earliest knowledge of what the Genesee area was like originally is pretty well described by my father. Speaking of riding from our place six miles east of Genesee to Lewiston on a saddle horse without crossing a fence, and with the bunchgrass rubbing the horse's belly all the way down to the lower part of the Lewiston Canyon. This is something that I came too late to see. I was born in 1911, so my memory of that country, of course, is all fences and plowed ground and mud roads. Mud roads are a thing that very few people now experience, unless they make the wrong turn in the spring of the fall in our area and leave the gravel road. Then they find out what mud roads are. I was on the end of a mud road six miles away from the nearest town, and for certain stages of the year in this country, that's isolation. Your trips to town were a thing. Were infrequent enough that you considered that quite an event when you were a child in those days.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: There was one period of the year when you got to go to town often enough that it could have become monotonous, were it not for this contrast of the long period of isolation, the time you got to go to town when the crops were hauled into town and delivered. And then you got to go every day or maybe twice a day. And this was for a boy that was big enough to enjoy it and too small to work. This was quite an enjoyable thing.

You must realize that all of the heating of our homes for the winter was accomplished by burning wood, and in the Genesee area there never was any great amount of wood very close. Some of the canyons had trees in some of. Frequently some of this was used as fuel. And in my day it was supplemented somewhat. If you hadn't accumulated a big enough woodpile, it would be supplemented sometimes by buying coal in the nearest town. This coal, you can see, almost had to be hauled before the roads deteriorated to the point where it became a pretty difficult thing. So after the harvest, one of the last things you did in the fall was to try to accumulate enough wood to see you through another year of farming. You might think of this as a hardship, but really, this was like a vacation. We got our wood from Troy, or north of Troy, or near Troy, and one of the places where we frequently got the wood was from Oak Olson's sawmill, which was within, what would you say, a mile or two of Troy?

Another thing I liked about the wintertime. We had no refrigeration, remember? So the wintertime was the time when we butchered and there would be butchering day, maybe several, maybe more than one. words: I think that that happened all the time. Probably happens, probably still occurs all the time. I know that there was always frequently rebellion between one generation and the other. I think it's probably inescapable. But I think we have lost a thing that probably now explains why this rebellion is so exaggerated and in many instances, so unjustified now, wherein it wasn't so extreme previously. And that is that in those more or less primitive days, everyone could see the necessity of the things that they were expected to do, maybe not at in infancy, but as they developed and got older, as the case that I pointed out, the wood did look good to me and did feel good to me when I was toasting my shins over it that winter. But the day that I was unloading it, when I would rather have been doing something else, it didn't look quite that good.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Now… a kid that is going to school comes home to a house where his father is away working, his mother is away working, and there is no chores that this kid has to do. There is nothing to feed. There's no cow to go out and get out of the fields to bring in for milking. There's maybe nothing but a cat, a house cat, a spoiled house cat to pet or stroke, or to greet you when you come home… i've heard my wife speak of a boy who described the dog as he came home, coming out laughing to greet him. There was something to come home to, and there was something that you were going to do when you got home. And I can remember when I was in grade school getting big enough so that as I walked home, I would pass the field where my father was working and if I hurried after school, let out, maybe ran a little, which I frequently did, I could get to this field before my father would have disappeared from sight on the other side of the field, get there in time so that I could take his team… and drive it around the field until quitting time, and he could go home and rest or get the cows, which I was getting pretty tired of doing, and I would feel pretty good about this. I felt pretty big about this. I felt like I was a pretty accomplished person to be trusted out there with a team of 6 or 8 horses.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I at that time, kind of missed the significance of the fact that I was driving a team that had just worked almost a whole day and worked really much in the mood to run away, but still, this was part of growing up. I could see the necessity then of everything I did. I was learning a skill as I grew along. I never did in my life reach a point where I thought, well, here I am and I don't know anything to do to make a living. I also was never in the point of so many of the younger people now that have been educated to a certain point, and they're pretty much afraid that their time is too valuable for them to accept the wage that they can get at the job that is available to them. I knew what these jobs were worth. I didn't expect to get paid the same as the hired man, unless I could do the same thing as the hired man did.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: One thing along this line a little incident. It isn't big by itself, but when I was too little to really be of much help, we harvested grain by binding it into bundles by a machine called a binder. The the outgrowth of Cyrus McCormick's Reaper. It was a machine that cut and tied, cut the grain and tied it into sheaves. Pretty heavy, pretty big. And part of the curing and ripening process of this method of harvest was that the grain could be cut green before it shattered out, and then it had. These sheaves had to be stood up on end in a mutually supporting wigwam shape, which we call it a shock. We were shocking grain, shocking bundles. And this is pretty hard work, and it required a certain amount of skill. And the first time you do it, they won't stand up, they'll fall down. And that shock isn't shocked. That shock is just a pile and a rain will spoil it. And you've got to do it over again. And my father paid me just to get me interested. A penny, a shock for shocking these bundles and.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: One of the hired man that was shocking. About 15 or 20 of these shocks to my one said to me, is your dad paying you for this? And I said, yes, he is. And I told him how much I got. And the hired man says, God, I wish he'd pay me that much. I'd be getting about $15 a day. And you must remember that $15 a day in those days was a hell of a lot more money than anybody ever got. It was just like saying I'd be a millionaire. And I felt quite a bit of shame at this, because I had sort of been hoping for a little word of praise from this higher gram that I had pretty much admired, but that was how you got into it. You didn't really want to be paid for something you didn't do. You wanted to be a person that earned what you got to hired him to felt this way. They they took a pride they wanted. They wanted to have a reputation of being a good and efficient hired man. And this is the thing that would get them land when they wanted to rent it.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: They had a reputation in the neighborhood of being this kind of a man, and lots of people would go a little bit out of their way to push a good deal. And by that I mean a deal as being a good proposition. The way of this hired man that had a good reputation. There's a hired man that a neighbor of ours had right down in the Genesee area… now that… is in that position. And I don't know if he got his start that way, but I do know the son of his employer that. Said, one at one time remarked to me, there never will be anybody like this hired man again. And this particular hired man has prospered. And he's a farmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now about the hired men were did most the farms years. And when you were growing up and the surrounding ones did most of them have one permanent hired man during the year or how how was it done? Or would they sometimes have more than one or sometimes less than a year?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: All of those ways, all of those ways. Sometimes you had no hired man. Sometimes you had a chore boy. And he might even later become your hired man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A chore boy.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: This would be a boy, maybe from one of the towns, or maybe one of the neighbor's kids that would come to the farm and milk the cows, gather the eggs, split the wood, and hold the garden, do all the odd jobs, run errands and so forth for a wage before he was old enough or big enough to be trusted with a team of horses, or the more hazardous work. If he took this job as a chore boy, it might be in his mind, it was with the hopes of becoming a hired man, or if he was a boy from the neighboring town, it might be for the sake of becoming a good hired man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he live at at the house?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: He would live with you. I remember one time my father was in town, and he happened to be in the hardware store, and there was two little kids there now. Or what? He considered little kids, I think 11 and 12, something like that. And… they had… run away from home, I'd believe, or maybe more or less left home with the permission of their parents. And their names stick in my mind. I never knew them by anything but their first name, but the names were Oskar and Tally and the hardware men. They had run away to the west, you might say. Or run away to the to the country to get a job seeking out, to set there for setting out, to seek their fortune, as it were… and Iron Man said to my dad, haven't you got a place, Sherm, that you could use these boys? And my dad rather reluctantly,… he was a sympathetic man, and with some misgivings, I should say, said, well, I think I could use them and brought these two boys home. They were considerably and by considerably at that time, I mean probably two or 3 or 4 years older than I was. And so here the two kids showed up at our place to be chore boys.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And of course, there wasn't enough supervision to keep two boys of that age busy doing chores. And so my father contacted a neighbor who thought he could use one or the others, and those boys stayed there quite a while. I. Don't they never did come back to work as hired men, but at least they got a taste of farm life and probably learned a lot, and I imagine, looked back with some pleasure later on to the experiences they had had there, because they were city boys and didn't know anything about the country. This never happened again at our place because, well, I, I shouldn't say this because various sons of various relatives and so forth feel this same thing. Occasionally friends son would come out and do chores for board and room or for a small wage in order to live on a farm. Chores, after all, you must remember, was considered doing nothing and it was worth your board and room if you did enough of it. That's all the farmer got out of his chores was his board or part of his board.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And so why should the hired man have more? He was getting his board to. So, so much for the chore boy. They sometimes were from one source, sometimes from another. Sometimes it was the lowest rung on the ladder toward becoming a farmer. And usually as soon as a farmer's family got big enough. His son was the boy and he didn't expect any compensation for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, we were talking about the migrants and we got off the subject of the migrant working for us. And I would really like to to know a little more about about them and what you thought of them. Now, they were I, I, they were different kind of people, obviously, than the, than the guys who were, hired hands and who were more often local.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes. That was the distinction you'd have to make was that they were a different person. They weren't a local person. Although this body of people that I like to refer to as the migrant farm worker, you might have people born and raised in your community who were a part of this more or less nebulous workforce, the migrant worker, and he might show up at your place or a neighbor's place or something. You knew him. He was a neighbor. He was down someplace harvesting. He was up in Canada harvesting. You heard of him? His folks told you about him. But by and large, the men that we meet would be strangers in the first instance and then might later return year after year. When the harvest season came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Around, you would frequently have the same.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Edward Olson, Edward Olson, and Edward Olson, the Scandinavia, the Norwegian, and a man named Johnson, who was the Swede, showed up at our place 3 or 4 different years, maybe not consecutively, but one time or another. They had worked in this area before and had worked at a place where they had liked the the employer. Naturally, that would be the first place that they would apply for a job if the employer they had previously worked for didn't have a job, maybe he could suggest somebody that they would go to for a job. There never was any. Very seldom was there ever any correspondence ahead of time or even a phone call or anything. They just showed up at your gate someday, or you met them in town someday. And he asked you, are you going to need any help, this haying or this harvest? Why? That was how those arrangements were made oftentimes.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Or maybe I shouldn't say oftentimes, but it was not unexpected. If a man who had worked for you one time came to your house looking for employment, or to visit you as a friend and stay overnight and talk about working and whether he was going to work. And these were acquaintances. They were friends. You… there was… i would I would like to say there was no social differences between you, but I can't say that there was a feeling of some aloofness between you and this migrant farm worker. True. He ate at your table. He slept in your house or in your bunkhouse or in your barn. He became well acquainted with the family. But if he was a man that went to town on Saturday night and got drunk, this was none of your business. As long as he did his work, it was none of your business. If the farmer. Was in the living room discussing things with his family, the hired man somewhat.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Thoughtfully, or the. The hired man was sensitive enough that he didn't barge into this family room without an invitation, or without a reason, or without an invitation. Usually he was invited to come in and play cards or whatever you were doing or discuss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This would be true equally of hired men and migrant workers, or more so of migrant.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Workers, more so of migrant worker than the hired, than the man that you called your hired man. Oftentimes a hired man, the one that I like to think of as the hired man. Oftentimes he was he was just like a member of your family. He was probably closer to you than many of your relatives. And he knew your problems and you knew his and you sympathized with each other. This was your local, the hired man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So perhaps a farmer would feel free to give him advice or tell him, or be concerned about his welfare if he was going into town and and maybe didn't see me in the best of shape.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Unless the farmer was pretty much one to hit the cups himself, he wouldn't have ever become the hired man if he did this. That was the. There was a distinction between the hired man and the migrant worker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the migrant worker usually come as a single person or or did he come?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: They sometimes came as a pair of relatives of each other, not you, but of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Each other. But they didn't travel in in groups.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: They sometimes did. One time we had a pair of men come a. Man and his son in law, and they may they they made a point of working at the same place together. And the son in law was a splendid worker, and my father would have been glad to have kept him. And the father in law was probably a burden to this son in law, and really didn't hold up his end at all, in my father's estimation, and my father decided not to keep the father in law and the son in law very politely and calmly, with no rancor, said, well, I'd like to stay, but I can't. I'll have to quit too. And so they did, and in my estimation, probably this son in law was by this method, by doing the most work that he could do, was able frequently to maintain his father in law in a job where he would be tolerated because of the virtues of the Son of Law. I think my father appreciated this, but didn't feel that he was in a position to subsidize.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: He had his own. He had his own people that he was responsible to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these men made top wages when they were because they followed the harvest.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, the harvest season was a high praying season and the hard work, it was worth every bit they got. But, they worked long hours and.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Still somehow I have the maybe it was a little different than. But the my idea of the migrant, the farm labor force now is that is that they are just really at the bottom as far as their, their, their own social conditions.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: This has been this viewpoint has been brought about by several things, in my opinion. One, of course, is the Chavez effort to organize farm labor. And of course, Chavez is talking about the orchard labor and yes, Mexican laborer. And I don't know anything about it and can't touch on it because I only know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm not thinking about it now, really, I'm asking you whether really whether whether back then these men were economically.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I think it's a different thing. I think it's a different thing than one of this. Most of these farm laborers, or many of them had a hope of accumulating some money. Usually they didn't. They ended the thing that destroyed the farm laborer, in addition to the changing conditions, was the attitude of the draft during World War Two toward the person that didn't have a steady job. Somehow, in the type of bureaucracy, bureaucratic stupidity that we're faced with so frequently, they never did recognize the migrant farm labor as laborer, as being an essential, steady, hardworking man, which he was. To get any kind of a draft deferment, you had to have, first of all, a steady job. And that right there destroyed or probably hastened the destruction or the end of a migrant farm labor force. Although the change in the machinery type of machinery has done just as much or more to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I would I would certainly think that that I mean, if you were to choose that, that being a hired hand would be referable to being migrant, although you might get more money as a, as a migrant place to place the, the changing all the time and the additional problems and all that going with that would be pretty, pretty difficult. Although I do understand that that, for example, I've heard about plenty of tramp lumberjacks too.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, yeah, this is a part of the picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I suppose these folks were kind of kin in a way, because it's a related kind.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Of I think. So I think, though, that I made a mistake by calling our laborers migrant farm laborers, because although this does describe them, it would better to use a separate term and call them the harvest tramp, because harvest was what they followed. Harvest was what they did. They might work at other jobs when there was no harvest to do. I don't know what some of their was. They might have been doing anything. One man that worked at our place had his other field was cutting cloth for clothes somewhere. This was his background. Others might on occasion have started as seafaring men part of the time or might. This was what made them interesting. They might have originated in anything, or they might have other skills that you wouldn't expect. But the the harvest tramp he harvested and might do most anything else the rest of the year, but he made this harvest season stretch out for a long time by moving right.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: That's there's a distinction between the Harvest Man and the lumberjack, although oftentimes it was one and the same man. He might have done one for a while. He might have done the other for a while. I've some of them mind. I've on our plates. We've had miners that had quit mining and taken up this other life outside of the earth rather than underneath. They might have quit mining for a reason, for this very reason that they got tired of it. There was one man that worked for us, one of the last migrant workers that worked for us. His name was Buck Lewis. Red Lewis had a very good sense of humor, and he was describing some of the work that he had done other than farming, and he described some mining that he had done down in southern or Southern California. I believe it was in Earth that was not like rock. You bored holes in it with an organ. And I having seen calico in Southern California, I presume it was in a area like this, a kind of an earthy rock or a soil, and told me of how much he made there, which was about three times what he was getting at our place.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And it would be like now a man that here might be working for $3 an hour, putting it in the modern time and wage scale, I would say. Suppose he were working at our place at $3 an hour right now, and he would, in conversation, have said to me, well, I was making I was making $15 an hour, or I was making $10 an hour, and I couldn't afford to my fritter away my time like that, so I quit. Now that is the only explanation he ever gave to me as to why he had quit mining and started. Moving from farm to prime. Incidentally, this man had worked at our place two different times and one time this was during the depth of the depression. I offered him the. My father was dead by this time, offered him a chance to stay there all winter if he wanted to help me chore around doing nothing again. And we spent an enjoyable winter together with him in our family.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And he here's an instance of a farm laborer that just fit right in, just like the hired man would if he was the hired man there when he was at our place. And I think perhaps the thing that endeared him to us was his sense of humor, which my family shared with anyone who exposed it. I'll give you just a few little incidents, although this is far from being history. It did happen back in the depression days though, so that's quite a little while ago. The 30s. And along with this chalking around and doing nothing that we did there for quite a long period of time, every day when the weather would permit, we would drive to town with a team of horses, two teams of horses and two sleds, and get a load of feed for all these animals and haul it out to feed the feet, to fill the feed bins so that we would have it there when work started in the spring and we had to use these horses in the field.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: You don't feed horses grain unless you're working them, and this was in preparation to getting ready to work the horses. Although these that were doing the hauling, they got grain. But the their fellow horses that were going to eat it in the summer didn't get to eat it in the winter. And this was in real cold weather when the sledding was good and it was darn cold and we were going to or from town. I got off and walked as far as you frequently do when you're driving horses and are cold while you walk, so you keep warm. And Buck said to me as I was doing this, he says, well, I'll sit here and freeze like a man before I'll get off and walk like a dog. Another thing that might be a little side light on some of the lighter moments. He had one bad habit he chewed Copenhagen. This was almost a common habit to most farm workers. Harvest tramps. Smoking would be almost forbidden. They most all of them chewed tobacco or chewed Copenhagen for a vice. And this man was helping me build a machine shed that winter.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And so he was up on a ladder and with nailing some boards on and with his mouth full of nails. And I heard him mumbling through these nails, now got, dammit, you would want to chew a snooze. I can't remember too many of these little incidences.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like a very humorous.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: This is just typical of a relation that more often than not, I should say every time it was possible occurred between the farmer and his employee where you could. It lightened the day. It made it easier. It made it a pleasure. I think this is probably typical of what happened in many farm families, although as a farmer's son I never worked, but very little as a hired man. And so I can only speak firsthand of my own farm, my father's farm, and maybe of some of the close neighbors. But it would be typical of those, which. Sounds like that experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With sounds like when when things were were maybe ideal, as you say. But the better the the better relationships. You put all the differences aside, and there was just a camaraderie between the people to get work done.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: It's as I have tried to point out to a man that doesn't want to work for somebody else. He resents working for somebody else. I told him, look at it this way you're fighting a common enemy, your allies, a thing that I have felt, and I think that these hired men felt was the hired man performs a service for his employer, and the employer performs a service for his hired man. He. You provide him the tools of production, and he provides you the man to run them. You guarantee him an income, and he helps you try to win one at a more chancy game. I wish that there were better relations all through labor and management, labor relations that parallel this because this is a situation that does exist, and it is one that it is not. To the interest of the managements of labor unions to foster. I have seen an instance or two where I think the answer to it would be cooperative enterprises, where the labor force are the stockholders and some of the stockholders are the management where it is mutually shared.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: But you don't want philosophy, you want desperation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I think I ought to get.
